coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots induced in-situ formation of porous europium micro-networks with enhanced photocatalysis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yue
Chen, Bin-Bin
Gao, Ya-Ting
Jiang, Lei
Lv, Jian
Chang, Shuai
Qian, Ruo-Can
Li, Da-Wei
coredata.dc:description: Excellent photocatalysts are highly desirable for clean energy and pollutant treatment. Carbon dots (CDs)-based composite photocatalysts have been widely studied and utilized in catalytic fields. However, the preparation of the photocatalysts with high catalytic activity still faces a great challenge. Herein, white-light-driven CDs-based porous europium micro-networks (CDs@P-Eu-MNs) composite photocatalysts are prepared by a facile in-situ growth strategy. CDs can affect the morphology and produce a large number of porous structures of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Importantly, the introduction of CDs not only increases the light absorption, but also promotes the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus improve photocatalytic performance of CDs@P-Eu-MNs composites. CDs@P-Eu-MNs show the highest photocurrent density, which can be used for the highly-efficient photodegradation of rhodamine 6G dyes with almost 95% degradation rate under low power white light (20 W) without any radical generating agents such as H2O2. Therefore, this new and efficient CDs@P-Eu-MNs photocatalyst will have a great application prospect in water pollution treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Porous europium micro-networks
In-situ formation
Photocatalysis
Dye degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photodegradation process of R6G using CDs@P-Eu-MNs
Photocatalytic activity of CDs@P-Eu-MNs under white light

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology characterization of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (a, b) SEM images and (c) high-resolution SEM image of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (d, e, f) SEM image of focused ion beam section of CDs@P-Eu-MNs (inset: SAED pattern). (g) Elemental mapping of focused ion beam section of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. White arrows referred to CDs. (h) XPS; (i) C1s; (j) O1s and (k) FT-IR spectra of CDs@P-Eu-MNs.

Optical properties of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (a) Emission spectrum of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (b) 3D FL spectrum of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (c) Absorption spectrum of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (d) FL confocal images of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Excitation wavelength: 405 nm.

Photocatalytic activities of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (a) Transient photocurrent responses for CDs, Eu-MNs and CDs@P-Eu-MNs. (b) Photocatalytic degradation of R6G in the presence of CDs@P-Eu-MNs under the white light irradiation (20 W). R6G: 20 μM. F represents the FL intensity in different degradation time. F0 represents the FL intensity at the beginning of the degradation reaction. (c) Photodegradation efficiency of R6G on CDs@P-Eu-MNs by the addition of different radical scavengers. Concentration of AO and EDTA-2Na scavengers is 50 mM. Concentration of BQ scavenger is 10 mM. Concentration of IPA scavenger is 1 M. (d) Photodegradation of R6G in the presence of 10 mM HCl or NaOH solution. F represents the FL intensity in different degradation time. F0 represents the FL intensity at the beginning of the degradation reaction. ESR spectra of radical adducts trapped by DMPO (
OH and
O2 −) in CDs@P-Eu-MNs in methanol dispersion for (e) DMPO-
O2 − and (f) in aqueous dispersion for DMPO-
OH.
Schematic illustration of white-light-driven photocatalytic process of CDs@P-Eu-MNs.
Schematic diagram of photocatalytic mechanism of CDs@P-Eu-MNs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots induced in-situ formation of porous europium micro-networks with enhanced photocatalysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center
Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry
East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots-based porous europium micro-networks (CDs@P-Eu-MNs) are prepared by a facile in-situ growth strategy, which can be used for the highly-efficient photodegradation of rhodamine 6G dyes with almost 95% degradation rate under low power white light (20 W).

Excellent photocatalysts are highly desirable for clean energy and pollutant treatment. Carbon dots (CDs)-based composite photocatalysts have been widely studied and utilized in catalytic fields. However, the preparation of the photocatalysts with high catalytic activity still faces a great challenge. Herein, white-light-driven CDs-based porous europium micro-networks (CDs@P-Eu-MNs) composite photocatalysts are prepared by a facile in-situ growth strategy. CDs can affect the morphology and produce a large number of porous structures of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Importantly, the introduction of CDs not only increases the light absorption, but also promotes the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and thus improve photocatalytic performance of CDs@P-Eu-MNs composites. CDs@P-Eu-MNs show the highest photocurrent density, which can be used for the highly-efficient photodegradation of rhodamine 6G dyes with almost 95% degradation rate under low power white light (20 W) without any radical generating agents such as H2O2. Therefore, this new and efficient CDs@P-Eu-MNs photocatalyst will have a great application prospect in water pollution treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalysts are considered as ideal materials for applications in energy and the treatment of pollutants.[1–3] The conventional photocatalysts such as titanium dioxide (TiO2), bismuth phosphate (BiPO4) and bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6) have been limited for the photodegradation of dyes because of weak light absorbance and fast charge recombination.[4–6] For example, TiO2 are mainly excited at the UV light region, and only can absorb 3% of the total solar spectrum because of its wide band gap (3.2 eV).[7] Similarly, the photocatalytic performance of Bi2WO6 is poor due to its narrow photo-absorption and fast electron-carriers recombination.[8] Therefore, newly designed photocatalysts are usually required to have strong and wide light absorption capacity, and the high separation efficiency of photoexcited electrons and holes.

In recent years, composite or hybrid photocatalysts have been widely studied and developed for photocatalytic degradation of dyes under white light irradiation.[9–11] The hybrid photocatalysts usually show an enhanced photocatalysis activity because an intimate interface can effectively improve the separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs.[12–14] Meanwhile, the photocatalysis under white light has a better application prospect as compared to monochromatic photocatalysis such as UV light because white light simulates natural sunlight. However, the light source with power up to several hundred watts in the current photocatalysis system is necessary because it is difficult for low energy light source to produce photogenerated electrons and holes.[15–17] Therefore, the highly-efficient photocatalysis in low power white light is urgent and meaningful for the treatment of pollutants.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, which show great potential in the field of photocatalysis because of their unique chemical stability and excellent electron transfer ability.[18–20] On the basis of these advantages, CDs as a support can be well used to the construction of CDs-based hybrid photocatalysts, such as CDs/TiO2 [21], CDs/Pt[22], and CDs/g-C3N4 [23] composites, by the physical mixing, chemical bonding, and in-situ growth methods[2]. Among three synthesis methods, in-situ growth is regarded as the heterogeneous nucleation and growth of the other nanostructure on the surface of CDs. The forming hybrid photocatalysts by in-situ growth are more stable and can promote the separation efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes at their interfaces.[24] Meanwhile, CDs-based hybrid photocatalysts can also enhance light utilization efficiency because of the strong absorption ability of CDs, therefore, the design of CDs-based hybrid photocatalysts in photocatalytic applications is very attractive.

In this work, we prepare CDs-based porous europium micro-networks (CDs@P-Eu-MNs) composite by a facile hydrothermal method. The CDs have been used as a good support for the nucleation and growth of the pristine nanomaterials. Interestingly, in the absence of CDs, only europium micro-networks (Eu-MNs) without porous structure are formed, indicating that CDs are the direct reason of the porous structure production. This may be due to the release of water molecules that come from the dehydration reaction between the abundant amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs and the carboxyl group of the Eu-MNs. The construction of CDs@P-Eu-MNs not only extends the absorption to the visible region, but also promotes the transfer and separation of photogenerated electrons. Meanwhile, the efficient fluorescence (FL) emission makes the electrons and holes in CDs@P-Eu-MNs can be excited under weak light. Considering the good optical properties, CDs@P-Eu-MNs have been well used for the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine 6G (R6G) dyes with a degradation rate of almost 95% under low power white light (20 W) without using any radical generator reagents (Scheme 1 ).

Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) is purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Ammonium oxalate (AO), europium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·6H2O), isopropanol (IPA), 1,4-benzoquinone (BQ), oxalic acid (OA), cysteine (Cys), citric acid (CA) and R6G are from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. All solutions are prepared using 18.2 MΩ.cm ultrapure water. All commercial chemicals are used without any further purification.

The morphology images of CDs@P-Eu-MNs are obtained by GeminiSEM 500 field-emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). High-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM) images and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CDs@P-Eu-MNs are obtained on Talos L120C TEM. UV–vis absorption spectra are collected by Lambda 950 UV–visible-near infrared spectrophotometer. FL spectra are obtained on a LS-55 Lumine spectrophotometer. The element composition of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is measured with ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs@P-Eu-MNs are collected on a FT-IR spectrometer. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra are obtained with a Bruker ESR-300E spectrometer.

CDs are firstly prepared by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.1 g CA and 0.1 g Cys are dissolved in 6 mL of deionized water and stirred for 1 min to form the mixture solution. Then the solution is transferred into a 25 mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 120 °C for 5 h. Finally, the resulting product is transferred to a clean centrifuge tube (0.1 mg/mL) and stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for further use.

For the synthesis of CDs@P-Eu-MNs, 100 μL CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL), 0.8 g CA and 0.4 g Eu(NO3)3·6H2O are dissolved in 4.9 mL deionized water and stirred for 1 min. The obtained solution is transferred into a 25 mL Teflonlined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The autoclave is cooled down naturally and followed by purification. Because of the micrometer size, CDs@P-Eu-MNs cannot be dispersed in aqueous solution and is easy to precipitate. Thus, the resulting products are precipitated and suspended for 5 times with distilled water. Subsequently, the final products are dried by vacuum drying oven to obtain clean CDs@P-Eu-MNs powder for further utilization. As a control, Eu-MNs are prepared using CA and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O as the precursors in the absence of CDs. All other conditions are consistent with the synthesis of CDs@P-Eu-MNs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photodegradation process of R6G using CDs@P-Eu-MNs
ce:section.ce:para: The FL intensities of R6G at different photodegradation time are measured by a FL spectrophotometer. CDs@P-Eu-MNs powder (75 mg) is firstly added into the clean glass bottle, and R6G aqueous solution (1 mL, 20 μM) is further added. The above mixture solution is irradiated under the white light (20 W, Xe light) within 150 min. The excitation wavelength for measuring the FL of R6G is set as 490 nm.

In order to synthesize CDs@P-Eu-MNs, CDs are firstly prepared by a one-pot hydrothermal method. The as-prepared CDs are homogeneous in size with about 3 nm, and own monodisperse morphology with an amorphous structure (Figure S1). The CDs in CDs@P-Eu-MNs composites can efficiently promote photogenerated charge transfer.[8] Meanwhile, the CDs can be used as an excellent support for the nucleation and growth of the pristine nanomaterials. The production yield of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is 21.4% (Figure S2), indicating that the established method has the advantages of being facile and gram-scale.

SEM image of CDs@P-Eu-MNs clearly shows that they possess a spatially well-defined 3D network structure (Fig. 1 a). Enlarged image (Fig. 1b) further reveals that these “hot-nodes” of the 3D network with about 10 μm are well connected with a stick-like junction. Meanwhile, the surface of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is rich in rough porous structure (Fig. 1c). SEM images (Fig. 1d and e) of focused ion beam section show that the internal structure of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is also porous with a pore diameter of about 50 nm (Fig. 1f). Interestingly, the precursors (CA and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O) only can be hydrothermally carbonized into Eu-MNs without porous structure in the absence of CDs during the synthesis (Figure S3). This result suggests that in addition to being used as supports, CDs can also produce a large number of porous structures in CDs@P-Eu-MNs, which may be due to the release of water molecules that originate from the dehydration reaction between the amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs and the carboxyl group of the Eu-MNs. The porous property increases the specific surface area of the CDs@P-Eu-MNs hybrid photocatalysts, and thus effectively promotes the adsorption of CDs@P-Eu-MNs and dye molecules. The SAED pattern (Fig. 1f, inset) shows a diffused halo ring, suggesting that CDs@P-Eu-MNs are a non-crystalline structure.[25] Meanwhile, the broad diffraction peak at about 43° in XRD spectrum further confirms that CDs@P-Eu-MNs are an amorphous structure (Figure S4). Thermogravimetric curve (Figure S5) of CDs@P-Eu-MNs shows a slow weight loss below 300 °C corresponding to the loss of physisorbed and coordinated water molecules. Even at a high temperature of 800 °C, CDs@P-Eu-MNs still have 44% by weight, indicating that they have a good thermal stability.

The elemental mapping of focused ion beam section shows that CDs@P-Eu-MNs contain Eu, O, C, N, and S atoms (Fig. 1g). These elements are evenly distributed in the CDs@P-Eu-MNs. S atom is the characteristic element of CDs, which can be used to trace the distribution of CDs in CDs@P-Eu-MNs. The bright blue particles corresponding to the CDs clearly reveal that CDs are uniformed distributed in the CDs@P-Eu-MNs. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1h) of CDs@P-Eu-MNs shows that C1s, O1s, S2p, Eu3d and Eu4d peaks are located about at 284.81, 531.53, 168.33, 1134.67 and 135.18 eV, respectively.[26–28] High-resolution C1s and O1s spectra (Fig. 1i and j) show that CDs@P-Eu-MNs contain abundant carboxylate, suggesting

that there is a strong coordination between CA and Eu3+ ions during the synthesis of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Moreover, FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1k) of CDs@P-Eu-MNs shows strong characteristic absorptions of the OH stretching vibrations at 3395 cm−1 and CO stretching vibrations at 1251 cm−1.[29] Importantly, two strong absorption peaks appeared at 1420 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 corresponding to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the carboxylate, further indicating the coordination between carboxyl groups and Eu3+ ions.[30] Therefore, the formation of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is based on the coordination between CA and Eu3+ ions.

FL spectrum (Fig. 2 a) clearly reveals that CDs@P-Eu-MNs have a broad emission peak in the blue region with the center at about 445 nm, which is attributed to the emission of CDs (Figure S6). Meanwhile, CDs@P-Eu-MNs can also emit typical Eu3+ transitions, including 5D0 - 7F4 (697 nm), 5D0 - 7F3 (652 nm), 5D0 - 7F2 (616 nm), and 5D0 - 7F1 (592 nm) transitions. Among these emission peaks, the most intense emission is originated from the 5D0 - 7F2 transition at 616 nm (Fig. 2b). Because of the parity forbidden 4f transition, Eu3+ ions can hardly be directly excited.[31] However, Eu3+ ions in CDs@P-Eu-MNs can be effectively excited, which is due to the symmetry deviation induced transition effect.[32] The formation of CDs@P-Eu-MNs weakens the coordination environment symmetry of Eu3+ ions, which causes the deviation of Eu3+ from the center of inversion, thus activates the originally Laporte-forbidden f-f transition. The absorption spectrum (Fig. 2c) of CDs@P-Eu-MNs shows multiple sharp absorption peaks, further suggesting that the parity forbidden 4f transitions are allowed after the formation of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Therefore, CDs@P-Eu-MNs show strong FL emission with blue and bright red colors under the excitation wavelength of 405 nm (Fig. 2d), exhibiting a multi-color imaging capability. Moreover, the 3D FL image (Figure S7) shows that CDs@P-Eu-MNs are spatially well-defined fluorescent assemblies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of CDs@P-Eu-MNs under white light
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the hybrid structure of CDs@P-Eu-MNs, the electrons generated by photoexcitation can be transferred rapidly, which promotes the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.[33] Meanwhile, the high FL efficiency indicates that CDs@P-Eu-MNs can be excited under the weak white light. Therefore, CDs@P-Eu-MNs show a great potential in white light photocatalysis because of their strong light absorption and excellent electron transfer capability.

The photocatalytic performances of CDs@P-Eu-MNs with different contents are firstly studied under the irradiation of white light. The most appropriate amount of CDs@P-Eu-MNs used for the photodegradation of R6G is 75 mg (Figure S8). The photocurrent-time (I - t) curve of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is shown in Fig. 3 a. The photocurrent intensity of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is stronger than that of CDs and Eu-MNs. In addition, the photocurrent intensity sharply increases once the white light is applied, which can reach a steady state during multiple intermittent “on–off” irradiation cycles. The FL spectra of R6G are recorded in different degradation times (Figure S9). With the increase of photodegradation time, the R6G dye is almost completely degraded. As shown in Fig. 3b, R6G almost is not degraded in the first 30 min, indicating that R6G molecules are mainly adsorbed on the surface of CDs@P-Eu-MNs catalyst in this stage. A great increase of degradation rate is observed in the next 115 min, and the degradation rate reaches nearly 95% when the degradation time is 160 min. The degradation of R6G dye experiences two pseudo first-order reaction processes with different reaction rates (Figure S10). After 30 min of adsorption, R6G began to undergo a photocatalytic process. In the next 45 min, the photocatalytic reaction constant is 0.0087 min−1. Subsequently, the catalytic degradation rate constant is further increased to 0.0373 min−1, showing a good photocatalytic activity. Meanwhile, the degradation efficiency still reaches 85% after five cycles of photocatalysis (Figure S11), implying that CDs@P-Eu-MNs catalyst has high stability and repeatability.

CDs@P-Eu-MNs as a highly-efficient photocatalyst show a better photocatalytic activity as compared to other common photocatalysts. As shown in Table S1, in order to generate a large amount of reactive oxygen species for degradation of dyes, three strategies are used in the current photocatalytic systems: (i) applying the UV light with high energy; (ii) using white light but need high power; and (iii) adding radical generating agents such as H2O2. These strategies are far from the goal of using sunlight to achieve photocatalytic degradation of dyes. In our experiment, CDs@P-Eu-MNs can efficiently degrade R6G dyes only under 20 W white light without any radical generating agents.

Because the parity forbidden 4f transitions of Eu3+ ions are allowed, CDs@P-Eu-MNs exhibit a strong light absorption containing the whole visible light region. Moreover, the hybrid structure of CDs@P-Eu-MNs can efficiently promote the separation of photogenerated charge carriers. These characteristics make CDs@P-Eu-MNs have a good ability of photocatalytic degradation of R6G dyes. Furthermore, the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs@P-Eu-MNs for the degradation of R6G dye is studied in detail. Radicals scavenger experiment reveals that the role of the active oxygen species on the photodegradation over CDs@P-Eu-MNs. As shown in Fig. 3c, the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs@P-Eu-MNs greatly decreases after adding the AO and EDTA-2Na solution (h+ scavenger), indicating that the h+ is extremely important for the degradation of R6G dye.[1] Meanwhile, the addition of BQ (
O2 − scavenger) significantly minimizes the photocatalytic efficiency of CDs@P-Eu-MNs, indicating that
O2 − also plays an important role in the photocatalysis process. But the
OH has a negligible effect on the photocatalytic activities of CDs@P-Eu-MNs because the high concentration of IPA (
OH scavenger) will not affect the photocatalytic efficiency. The different degradation efficiency of R6G in different acid/base solutions further confirms this above results (Fig. 3d). In acidic solution, the photocatalytic activity of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is almost unchanged. However, the degradation of R6G is completely inhibited under base condition because OH− can react with photogenerated h+ to produce
OH.[5] These results strongly prove that h+ is a sort of important catalytic active species.

To further reveal the photocatalytic mechanism, the ESR spin-trapping technique is used to measure the types of reactive oxidative species. The results show that a large amount of
O2 − (Fig. 3e) and a small amount of
OH (Fig. 3f) produced in the photocatalytic process. Based on the above discussion, a brief description of the photocatalytic process is shown as follow:
(1)
CDs @ P - E u - M N s + w h i t e l i g h t ®
h
+
+
e
-
(2)
O 2
+
e
-
® Â ·
O 2
-
In addition, the photocatalytic mechanism of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is shown in Scheme 2 . CDs@P-Eu-MNs produce photoexcited h+ and e− under the irradiation of white light. Then,
O2 − radicals can be formed by the combination of e− with O2 adsorbed on the surface of CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Furthermore,
O2 − can degrade R6G dyes into smaller molecules such as CO2 and H2O.[34] Importantly, h+ can also directly oxidize and degrade the R6G. Combination with radical scavenger experiment, h+ is the important factor in the photodegradation process of R6G by CDs@P-Eu-MNs. Therefore, the efficient photocatalytic activity of CDs@P-Eu-MNs is directly attributed to the highly active h+ and
O2 −.

In conclusion, we develop a facile strategy for the construction of CDs@P-Eu-MNs composite photocatalysts. CDs@P-Eu-MNs have good FL emission ranged from blue to red colors because of the enhanced light absorption. Moreover, the introduction of CDs also promotes the separation of photogenerated charge carriers, and improve photocatalytic performance of CDs@P-Eu-MNs composites. Because of the excellent optical properties, CDs@P-Eu-MNs show a strong photocatalytic activity for R6G degradation under white light irradiation. The CDs@P-Eu-MNs can efficient photocatalytic degradation of R6G dyes only under 20 W white light without any radical generating agents. The h+ and
O2 − are the main active species in the photodegradation process. The excellent reusability and stability make the CDs@P-Eu-MNs be very promising for the environmental protection.

Yue Wang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Bin-Bin Chen: Visualization, Investigation. Ya-Ting Gao: Investigation. Lei Jiang: Investigation. Jian Lv: Investigation. Shuai Chang: Investigation. Ruo-Can Qian: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Da-Wei Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21788102, 21777041, 21974046, 21977031), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (Grant No. 19391901700, 19520744000, 19ZR1472300), National Science and Technology Major Project of China (2018ZX10302205) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.08.057.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of indometacin by simulated sunlight activated CDs-loaded BiPO4 photocatalyst: Roles of oxidative species

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.X. Zhang, P. Chen, M.H. Zhuo, F.L. Wang, Y.H. Su, T.S. Chen, K. Yao, Z.W. Cai, W.Y. Lv, G.G. Liu, Degradation of indometacin by simulated sunlight activated CDs-loaded BiPO4 photocatalyst: Roles of oxidative species, Appl. Catal. B-Environ. 221 (2018) 129-139.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot-based composites for catalytical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.B. Chen, M.L. Liu, C.Z. Huang, Carbon dot-based composites for catalytical applications, Green Chem. 22 (2020) 4034-4054.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dendritic CuSe with hierarchical side-branches: Synthesis, efficient adsorption, and enhanced photocatalytic activities under daylight

Kim, Visible-light active nanohybrid TiO2/carbon photocatalysts with programmed morphology by direct carbonization of block copolymer templates, Green Chem. 13 (2011) 3397-3405.
